text: a Disney World vacation is no cheap

however it wasn't always that way back
when Disney World opened in 1971 an
adult 11:00 ride ticket book cost 575
and a room at the contemporary cost up
to forty four dollars a night even
adjusted for inflation that would come
out to around thirty four fifty for the
tickets and two hundred and sixty four
dollars for the hotel in reality a
one-day Magic Kingdom ticket costs a
hundred and thirty two dollars with tax
and a night at the contemporary facing
the theme park can run around six
hundred and seventy five dollars at
night so what happened when did Disney
World get so expensive well the quick
and short answer is that CEO Michael
Eisner and CFO Gary Wilson happened the
long answer is that circumstances at the
time really made the decision simple and
it's kind of hard to vilify them for
what was otherwise a sound business
movie
you see Disney as a company was in a bit
of a rough spot when Eisner took over as
CEO with the death of Walt Disney in the
later half of the 1960s the 1970s were
somewhat of a lost era for the company
sure today we can look back at projects
like Robin Hood and the rescuers and see
them as classics but the truth is the
company wasn't growing financially
during this time a company once known
for constant innovation at the hand of
Walt Disney quickly became one that
relied on its old tricks
due to a what would Walt do mentality
this lack of evolution and changes was
partially beneficial for fans as it was
an era in which admission prices at the

annual increases ranged from 50 cents to
a dollar or so as CEO at the time Card
Walker put it we have to keep our prices

Walt's own approach to the parks profit
was secondary to making something people
loved unfortunately
corporate raiders on Wall Street didn't

long story short however it meant a

Frank Wells even before officially
signing on as CEO Eisner saw the value
that was being underutilized at the
Disney parks in order to keep away any
future Wall Street Raiders Disney needed
to start improving their bottom line and
fast Eisner brought in a new chief
financial officer from Marya named Gary
Wilson and without any hesitation ticket
prices started to go up just to give you
an idea of the rate and the amount of
the increases consider this just two
years prior to Michael Eisner joining
the company Disney World ticket prices
rose twice for a total of $3 from $15
for a one-day ticket to $18 in the two
years following his arrival tickets rose
in price five times
jumping $8 to $26 the surprising part
for Disney was that even with the
frequent price hikes attendance wasn't
dropping proving Eisner right there was
more value in the admission to the
Disney parks than Disney was realizing
the new Disney leadership also saw a
value that was going unrecognized
elsewhere specifically in hotels on
property and VHS releases of Disney
classic films the company similarly
cashed in on those opportunities and by
1987 the company's operating income
jumped from just under 300 million
dollars to nearly eight hundred million
dollars the turnaround kept traders at
bay prices continued to rise in the
1990s and early 2000s even into Bob
iger's tenure as CEO over the last ten
years tickets would see annual hikes
that leave us at the prices we have
today now some might argue that Disney
is very different from the state it was
in 30 years ago and so they should stop
raising prices however even though
Disney improved drastically they weren't
always totally in the clear for instance
as late as 2004 Disney was the target of
another takeover bid this time by
Comcast for better or for worse the
growth focused mindset of Wall Street
and the constant looming threat of being
just willingly or otherwise by a bigger
fish means companies like Disney are
forced to try and grow every single year
this means more revenue this means
higher prices to get there so ultimately
Disney World is so expensive because
historically the price hikes were
crucial to elevating the company as a

is because when all is said and done
